content.text: GenAI integration work — writing system prompts, integrating guardrails, building eval harnesses, wiring LLM API calls — benefits from dedicated pairing practices that differ from standard driver/navigator pair programming. The primary difference is that the output being reviewed is probabilistic: the same code can produce different model responses on consecutive runs, and the evaluation of those responses requires both a product lens (does this help the user?) and a safety lens (could this harm anyone?). Assigning these two lenses to two seats makes invisible assumptions visible.

attrs.alt: GenAI pair programming session flow — Seat A (Prompt Author), Shared Eval Loop, Seat B (Safety Reviewer), rotation every 25 minutes

content.text: Why Standard Pairing Patterns Fall Short

In conventional pairing, the navigator reviews code logic and suggests improvements while the driver types. This works well for deterministic code where both people can trace through the execution path. GenAI integration introduces three properties that standard pairing does not account for:
Non-determinism
: two different prompt revisions might both pass a quick manual check but diverge on edge cases. A single navigator glance at the output is insufficient — you need a structured sample review.
Dual evaluation axes
: every output must be evaluated for helpfulness AND safety. A single reviewer trying to track both simultaneously tends to anchor on one, usually helpfulness.
State accumulation

: prompt engineering is an iterative process where each revision builds on the previous. Without a structured commit cadence, the pair loses track of which change caused which improvement or regression.
The solution is to give each seat a distinct, non-overlapping responsibility, and to make the eval loop the shared checkpoint between the two seats.
The Two-Seat Model

Each session has exactly two roles. Roles rotate on a fixed cadence (25-minute Pomodoro intervals work well for prompt engineering, where each iteration cycle — draft, call, review — takes 5–10 minutes).
Role
Primary Responsibility
Secondary Responsibility
Does NOT Do
Seat A — Prompt Author
Writes and revises system prompt; calls the LLM API; commits changes
Explains intent to the reviewer before each API call
Does not evaluate safety unilaterally; does not merge without reviewer sign-off
Seat B — Safety Reviewer
Challenges outputs for safety violations; notes adversarial inputs to test; flags scope creep
Maintains a running list of edge cases to add to the eval suite after the session
Does not touch the keyboard during the other person's seat; does not approve correctness (only safety)
Why separate axes matter:
when the same person evaluates both helpfulness and safety, they tend to justify safety issues away when the output is helpful. The two-seat model makes safety a veto, not a consideration.
Session Setup Protocol

Before the first Pomodoro begins, the pair runs through a 10-minute setup checklist. This prevents common session anti-patterns like agreeing on scope mid-session or discovering missing dependencies during the first API call.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum


class SessionGoal(Enum):
    WRITE_SYSTEM_PROMPT     = "write_system_prompt"
    INTEGRATE_GUARDRAIL     = "integrate_guardrail"
    BUILD_EVAL_HARNESS      = "build_eval_harness"
    REVIEW_EXISTING_PROMPT  = "review_existing_prompt"
    WIRE_LLM_API            = "wire_llm_api"


@dataclass
class PairingSessionSetup:
    goal: SessionGoal
    prompt_author: str          # name/handle of Seat A
    safety_reviewer: str        # name/handle of Seat B
    feature_id: str             # FEAT-XXXX from the register
    behavior_tier: str          # T0/T1/T2/T3 from the SIA
    scope_statement: str        # one sentence: what the prompt must and must not do
    out_of_scope: list[str]     # explicit list of topics the model must decline
    eval_dataset_path: str      # path to the eval JSONL file to run after each change
    rotation_minutes: int = 25

    def is_complete(self) -> bool:
        return all([
            self.scope_statement,
            self.out_of_scope,
            self.eval_dataset_path,
            self.prompt_author,
            self.safety_reviewer,
        ])

    def checklist(self) -> list[str]:
        """Items both people agree on before the first Pomodoro starts."""        return [
            f"Goal: {self.goal.value}",
            f"Scope: {self.scope_statement}",
            f"Out of scope ({len(self.out_of_scope)} items): {', '.join(self.out_of_scope[:3])}...",
            f"Behavior tier: {self.behavior_tier}",
            f"Eval dataset: {self.eval_dataset_path}",
            f"Rotation every: {self.rotation_minutes}min",
            f"Seat A (author): {self.prompt_author}",
            f"Seat B (reviewer): {self.safety_reviewer}",
        ]


# Example session for a nutrition assistant
session = PairingSessionSetup(
    goal=SessionGoal.WRITE_SYSTEM_PROMPT,
    prompt_author="alice",
    safety_reviewer="bob",
    feature_id="FEAT-2024-045",
    behavior_tier="T0",
    scope_statement=(
        "The assistant answers general nutrition questions about macro content and "        "dietary guidelines. It must decline personalised dietary advice, medical "        "conditions, and supplement dosing."
    ),
    out_of_scope=[
        "personalised calorie deficit for weight loss",
        "supplement dosing for specific conditions",
        "nutritional guidance for eating disorders",
        "medication-diet interactions",
    ],
    eval_dataset_path="eval/nutrition_v1.jsonl",
)
print(session.checklist())
During-Session Protocol
Each 25-minute Pomodoro follows the same four-step cycle. Breaking the cycle — skipping the eval run, or reviewing outputs individually before discussing — defeats the purpose of pairing.
Draft (5 min):
Seat A drafts or revises one section of the system prompt. Seat B is silent — they are reading the change and forming questions but not interrupting.
Explain (2 min):

Seat A narrates the intent of the change aloud: "I'm adding this because the model kept offering weight-loss advice when the user mentioned a target calorie count. This instruction should prevent that."
Shared eval (10–15 min):

Both people run the eval harness together on screen, then randomly sample 3–5 outputs that are near the boundary of the scope statement. Seat B challenges any output that looks borderline. They agree on whether the change improved or regressed behavior before moving on.
Commit + note (3 min):

Seat A commits the prompt change with a message referencing the specific behavior it targeted. Seat B notes any adversarial inputs discovered during the eval that should be added to the eval suite later.
Task-Specific Pairing Patterns
Different GenAI integration tasks have different pairing dynamics. The two-seat model adapts to each task type.
Task
Seat A Focus
Seat B Focus
Eval Checkpoint
Writing system prompt
Prompt instruction clarity; instruction ordering; tone
Out-of-scope coverage; refusal wording; scope creep
Run eval harness after every change; sample borderline outputs
Integrating input guardrail
Classifier threshold tuning; latency profiling
False positive rate; categories the classifier misses
Precision/recall on test set; latency p99
Building eval harness
Test case coverage; metric implementation
Coverage gaps; adversarial case inclusion
No automated eval — pair reviews test case quality manually
Wiring LLM API
Request/response structure; error handling; retries
Token logging; cost accounting; secrets handling
Integration test; token count assertion; error injection test
Reviewing existing prompt
Identifies unintended instructions; redundancies
Maps each instruction to a behavior; finds gap between intent and instruction
Diff the eval harness before/after removing each suspected redundancy
Handling Disagreements During the Session
The most common disagreement is whether an output is borderline-acceptable or a safety concern. A clear decision rule prevents the pair from spending a Pomodoro arguing about a single output.

class DisagreementResolution(Enum):
    REVIEWER_WINS    = "reviewer_wins"   # safety concern is presumed valid
    ADD_TO_EVAL      = "add_to_eval"     # add the disputed input to the eval dataset
    DEFER_TO_SIA     = "defer_to_sia"    # consult the Safety Impact Assessment for the behavior


def resolve_output_disagreement(
    author_assessment: str,   # "acceptable", "borderline", "reject"
    reviewer_assessment: str,
) -> DisagreementResolution:
    """
    When author and reviewer disagree on an output's acceptability,
    use this decision rule.
    """
    if reviewer_assessment == "reject":
        # Reviewer veto — safety concern always wins.
        return DisagreementResolution.REVIEWER_WINS

    if author_assessment == "acceptable" and reviewer_assessment == "borderline":
        # Genuine uncertainty — add to eval dataset; do not regress from the current prompt.
        return DisagreementResolution.ADD_TO_EVAL

    if reviewer_assessment == "borderline" and author_assessment == "borderline":
        # Both uncertain — consult the SIA to check if this behavior is in scope.
        return DisagreementResolution.DEFER_TO_SIA

    # Both agree
    return DisagreementResolution.ADD_TO_EVAL  # default: document it


# Practical rule: if the pair cannot agree in under 3 minutes, add the input
# to the eval dataset and move on. Do not spend a Pomodoro on one output.
End-of-Session Handoff
At the end of each pairing session, the pair produces a handoff record. This ensures the next engineer who picks up the work has the context to continue without starting over.
Commit Conventions for Paired Prompt Work

Standard commit conventions (feat:, fix:, chore:) do not capture enough context for prompt engineering changes, where the "why" of a change is as important as the "what". A team pairing on GenAI work should adopt an extended convention.

# Commit message convention for prompt engineering changes
#
# Format:
#   prompt(<behavior>): <what changed> [<eval delta>]
#   prompt-fix(<behavior>): <safety issue corrected>
#   guardrail(<behavior>): <guardrail added or tuned>
#   eval(<behavior>): <eval case added or modified>
#
# Examples:
git commit -m "prompt(nutrition): restrict scope to general facts; decline personalised advice [+4pp]"
git commit -m "prompt-fix(nutrition): explicit decline for eating disorder queries after reviewer flag"
git commit -m "guardrail(nutrition): add topic classifier for medical-condition keywords [p99 42ms]"
git commit -m "eval(nutrition): add 15 adversarial cases from session-3 pairing notes"

# The [+4pp] / [-2pp] suffix is the eval pass-rate delta from this change.
# Makes it easy to git log and see the contribution of each prompt change.
This convention makes
git log --oneline
a readable history of prompt evolution. When a regression appears in CI, the team can binary-search the commit log to find which prompt change caused it.
When to Break the Pairing Pattern
Pairing is a high-bandwidth collaboration mode. It is not always the right choice. Use this decision table to decide when pairing is worth the coordination cost.
Situation
Pair?
Reason
Writing a T0 or T1 system prompt for the first time
Yes — required
T0/T1 behaviors require a safety reviewer present; solo prompt authoring is not permitted for these tiers
Minor wording change to an existing T2 prompt
Async review
Low risk; Seat B reviews the PR diff rather than pairing live
Building the eval harness for a new behavior
Yes — recommended
Coverage gaps are easiest to catch with a second person challenging "what else could go wrong?"
Debugging a CI eval regression
Yes — recommended
Regressions in prompt behavior are often subtle; two people looking at sampled outputs catch the pattern faster
Refactoring prompt structure without changing semantics
No — solo
Pure structural change; verify with eval harness before + after; no safety risk if eval passes
Integrating a new LLM API client (no prompt changes)
No — solo
Deterministic code change; standard code review process applies
Measuring Session Effectiveness

After several sessions, the team should track whether pairing is improving prompt quality over time. Two metrics capture this without requiring extra instrumentation beyond the eval harness already in place.
Summary

GenAI pairing uses a two-seat model where Seat A (Prompt Author) drafts and commits prompt changes while Seat B (Safety Reviewer) challenges outputs for safety issues and accumulates adversarial test inputs. Roles rotate every 25 minutes on a Pomodoro cadence. Each rotation cycle includes a shared eval run, a sampled output review, a commit, and a note on new adversarial inputs. The model works because it separates the helpfulness lens from the safety lens — reviewer veto always wins when the pair disagrees.
Session setup
: agree on scope, out-of-scope list, eval dataset, and rotation cadence before the first Pomodoro
Pomodoro cycle
: Draft → Explain intent → Shared eval (3–5 sampled outputs) → Commit + note new adversarial inputs
Reviewer veto
: if the reviewer flags a safety concern, the change does not merge — no vote, no override
Disagreement rule
: if the pair cannot resolve in 3 minutes, add the input to the eval dataset and move on
Handoff record
: prompt file path, eval pass rate, open adversarial inputs, next steps, and known regressions
